summary: Gruber reflects on the retirement of Katie Cotton, Apple's long-time PR chief, by telling a personal anecdote from the 2010 Antennagate press event. He recounts how Cotton knew about his minor cold despite it being mentioned only in passing to a PR rep, illustrating her legendary attention to detail and information control. The piece frames Cotton as one of Apple's most powerful yet publicly invisible executives, responsible for building Apple's world-class internal PR operation. Gruber argues that Cotton played a central role in shaping Apple's brand mystique and controlled narrative, and her departure marks the end of an era.
key_points: Katie Cotton built Apple's internal PR team into a world-class operation, eschewing outside agencies entirely
The Antennagate press event was an unusually small, hastily organized Friday event — revealing how seriously Apple treated the crisis
Cotton's attention to detail was legendary: she tracked even minor personal details about journalists she worked with
Despite never appearing on Apple's executive leadership page, Cotton was among the company's most influential executives
Her departure signals a potential shift in Apple's famously controlled approach to media relations
money_quotes: Apple, famously, works hard to control the narrative surrounding its product releases, and generally does so successfully.
It was a 'this thing can't wait until Monday' situation — a fire that needed to be put out before it spread. Non-emergency press conferences are not held on Fridays.
Most big corporations employ outside PR agencies. Not Apple. Apple's internal public relations team is itself a world-class agency. That team was built entirely under Cotton's leadership.
Cotton was never pictured on Apple's executive leadership page, but my impression has long been that she was one of the very handful of most influential executives at the company.
Katie Cotton 'played a key role in shaping the mystique and exclusivity surrounding the Apple brand.'
key_insight: Katie Cotton's invisible but immense influence over Apple's public image — knowing everything, controlling every narrative — made her one of the company's most powerful executives despite never appearing on its leadership page.
